stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 10th.
stocks.news:
CoreWeave just locked down a massive $21 billion multi-year cloud deal with Meta — the GPU powerhouse now runs AI workloads for OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic, and adding Meta’s nearly $230 billion revenue engine to its roster is a major win.

Meanwhile, “Big Short” legend Michael Burry is placing a fresh bet — this time against Palantir. His thesis? AI tools could eventually make Palantir’s high-priced consulting model obsolete. Palantir shares are already down 28% in 2026, and even a Trump endorsement on Truth Social couldn’t stop the bleeding.

economy.commentary:
Consumer confidence just cratered to its lowest reading ever — the University of Michigan’s sentiment index set a new all-time low as inflation and high gas prices weigh on everyday Americans. Consumers now expect inflation to hit 4.8% by this time next year.

The data backs up the pessimism. Consumer prices surged 0.9% in March alone — the largest monthly spike since June 2022 — pushing annual inflation to 3.3%. Housing and energy costs led the way, with the Iran war keeping constant pressure on global markets.

On the geopolitical front, U.S.-Iran peace talks have stalled. President Trump is now threatening to blockade the Strait of Hormuz — a chokepoint that handles roughly a third of the world’s daily oil supply. WTI crude continues hovering near $100, and gold pulled back slightly as markets try to gauge the fallout.

crypto.commentary:
World Liberty Financial — the Trump-family-backed crypto venture — is in a tough spot. The project pledged 5 billion of its own tokens to borrow $75 million from decentralized lender Dolomite, but then its coin crashed 15% to a record low, erasing $427 million in market value. With $60 million in loans still outstanding, it’s an uncomfortable position.

Bitcoin itself has been treading water around $71,000 for a month straight. The Iran conflict is clouding any recovery story, and with inflation running hot, traders are losing hope the Fed will cut rates anytime soon. Analysts say BTC needs to reclaim $75,000 to reignite bullish momentum — a level it hasn’t touched in two months.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates holding steady at 6.39% — unchanged from last week as the 10-year yield ticks sideways.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 15-city tracker dipped to 2,585 — down 32 from Thursday. 248 coming soon and 266 new listings as the spring pipeline stays active.

📍 Fremont: 320 total with 36 new listings and 60 coming soon. Spring activity continues at a steady clip.

📍 Union City: Down one to 79 — still one of the tightest markets on the board.

📍 Oakland: 750 total with 67 new listings. The Bay’s biggest inventory pool keeps churning.

📍 Milpitas: 112 total with 31 coming soon but only 4 new. Heavy pipeline, light on fresh supply.

📍 Newark: Up to 104 with 5 new listings — one of the few gainers this cycle.

📍 Hayward: 222 total, down 26 from 248 — a noticeable pullback as pending sales absorbed a chunk of supply.

Nationally, the average first-time homebuyer is now 40 years old — up from the early 30s just 25 years ago. First-timers account for only 21% of all purchases, as a $300,000 home at today’s rates eats up 35-40% of household income.

Home builders are adapting as new construction growth is expected to hit just 1% this year. California’s backyard ADUs now make up nearly half of new single-family construction, Manhattan developers are converting offices into apartments, and manufactured homes are outpacing traditional builds. Remodeling is also surging as both buyers and sellers sit on the sidelines waiting for rates to move.
